questions.question: What is a game loop?
questions.answer: The heart of any game that continuously processes input, updates game state (physics, AI, logic), and renders graphics at a target frame rate (e.g., 60 FPS).

questions.question: What is the difference between fixed time step and variable time step in a game loop?
questions.answer: Fixed time step updates logic at constant intervals (stable physics), while variable time step uses delta time to move objects frame-rate independently (smoother movement).

questions.question: What is a game engine?
questions.answer: A software framework providing core functionalities like rendering, physics, audio, scripting, and asset management (e.g., Unity, Unreal Engine, Godot).

questions.question: What is the difference between Unity and Unreal Engine?
questions.answer: Unity uses C# and is popular for 2D/mobile/indie. Unreal uses C++/Blueprints, known for high-fidelity 3D/AAA visuals, and has better out-of-box rendering.

questions.question: What is a game object in Unity?
questions.answer: A fundamental container in Unity that holds components (Transform, Renderer, Collider, Scripts) to represent any entity in the game world.

questions.question: What is a MonoBehaviour in Unity?
questions.answer: Base class for Unity scripts providing lifecycle events: Start(), Update(), FixedUpdate(), OnCollisionEnter(), etc.

questions.question: What is a prefab in Unity?
questions.answer: A reusable asset template of a GameObject, allowing instantiation and batch updates across multiple instances.

questions.question: What is a Blueprint in Unreal Engine?
questions.answer: A visual scripting system allowing designers to create gameplay logic without coding, using nodes and connections.

questions.question: What is the difference between an Actor and a Component in Unreal?
questions.answer: Actor is an object placed in world (e.g., Character, Prop). Component provides functionality (Movement, Health, Mesh) attached to Actor.

questions.question: What is a scene (or level) in game development?
questions.answer: A container holding all game objects, environment, lighting, and logic for a specific area or level of the game.

questions.question: What is a shader?
questions.answer: A program running on GPU defining how vertices and pixels are processed to render materials, lighting, and special effects.

questions.question: What is the difference between a vertex shader and a fragment shader?
questions.answer: Vertex shader processes 3D vertices (position, normals). Fragment (pixel) shader determines final pixel color based on lighting, textures, and material.

questions.question: What is rendering pipeline?
questions.answer: The sequence of stages converting 3D scene into 2D image: vertex shading → rasterization → fragment shading → post-processing.

questions.question: What is a mesh?
questions.answer: A collection of vertices, edges, and faces defining the shape of a 3D object, typically created in modeling software (Blender, Maya).

questions.question: What is a texture?
questions.answer: A 2D image applied to a 3D model's surface to add color, detail, bumpiness (normal map), or reflectivity (specular map).

questions.question: What is UV mapping?
questions.answer: The process of unwrapping a 3D mesh onto a 2D plane (U,V coordinates) to map textures accurately onto the model.

questions.question: What is a normal map?
questions.answer: A texture encoding surface angle per pixel, faking high-resolution detail on low-poly models without adding geometry.

questions.question: What is LOD (Level of Detail)?
questions.answer: Using simpler models and textures for distant objects to optimize rendering performance, swapping as camera distance changes.

questions.question: What is occlusion culling?
questions.answer: A technique that prevents rendering objects blocked by others (e.g., inside a room), improving performance.

questions.question: What is frustum culling?
questions.answer: Discarding objects outside the camera's view frustum (viewable area) before rendering to save GPU time.

questions.question: What is ray tracing in game graphics?
questions.answer: Rendering technique simulating light rays (bounces, shadows, reflections) for photorealistic lighting, computationally expensive, hardware-accelerated in RTX GPUs.

questions.question: What is deferred rendering vs forward rendering?
questions.answer: Forward renders each object with lights in one pass. Deferred renders geometry first (G-buffer), then lighting in separate pass (handles many lights efficiently).

questions.question: What is a particle system?
questions.answer: A technique rendering thousands of small sprites (particles) for effects like fire, smoke, rain, explosions, each with individual position, velocity, lifespan.

questions.question: What is a sprite in 2D game development?
questions.answer: A 2D image (texture) drawn on screen, often from a sprite sheet (multiple frames) for animations and characters.

questions.question: What is a tilemap?
questions.answer: A grid-based level building technique using reusable tiles (16x16, 32x32) stored in a tileset, efficient for 2D platformers/RPGs.

questions.question: What is parallax scrolling?
questions.answer: A 2D camera technique where background layers move slower than foreground, creating depth and immersion in side-scrollers.

questions.question: What is a rigidbody (physics component)?
questions.answer: A component enabling game objects to respond to physics forces (gravity, collisions, drag) rather than manual movement.

questions.question: What is a collider?
questions.answer: A shape (box, sphere, capsule, mesh) attached to object for collision detection, optionally with triggers for events without physical response.

questions.question: What is the difference between continuous and discrete collision detection?
questions.answer: Discrete checks at discrete time steps (may miss fast objects). Continuous sweeps between frames (prevents tunneling) for fast-moving bullets/vehicles.

questions.question: What is a layer mask in Unity/Unreal?
questions.answer: Bitmask filtering which objects collide with each other or which layers a raycast ignores.

questions.question: What is a NavMesh (Navigation Mesh)?
questions.answer: Baked representation of walkable surfaces used by AI agents for pathfinding, avoiding obstacles dynamically.

questions.question: What is A* pathfinding algorithm?
questions.answer: Widely used pathfinding algorithm using heuristic (estimated cost) to find shortest path between nodes, balancing accuracy and performance.

questions.question: What is a state machine in game AI?
questions.answer: Behavior model where AI switches between states (Idle, Patrol, Chase, Attack) based on conditions, with transitions and actions per state.

questions.question: What is behavior tree?
questions.answer: Hierarchical AI structure with composite nodes (Sequence, Selector), decorators, and leaf actions, more modular than finite state machines.

questions.question: What is a finite state machine (FSM) vs behavior tree (BT)?
questions.answer: FSM transitions between states; BT re-evaluates from root each tick, more scalable for complex AI (e.g., enemy AI in AAA games).

questions.question: What is a trigger in game development?
questions.answer: An area collider causing events (OnTriggerEnter) without physics response, used for doors, pickups, checkpoints.

questions.question: What is a raycast?
questions.answer: Projecting invisible ray from point in direction, detecting hit objects. Used for shooting, line-of-sight, ground detection.

questions.question: What is a singleton pattern in game development?
questions.answer: Design pattern ensuring class has one global instance (e.g., GameManager, AudioManager), often used but can cause coupling.

questions.question: What is an object pool pattern?
questions.answer: Reusing objects (bullets, enemies) from pre-allocated pool instead of constant instantiation/destruction, reducing GC overhead.

questions.question: What is an event system (observer pattern) in games?
questions.answer: Decoupled communication where objects raise events (PlayerDied, ScoreChanged) and others subscribe, avoiding direct references.

questions.question: What is a coroutine in Unity?
questions.answer: A method allowing pauses (yield return) spanning multiple frames, useful for delays, animations, and asynchronous sequences.

questions.question: What is delta time?
questions.answer: Time elapsed between last and current frame, used to make movement speed frame-rate independent (velocity * deltaTime).

questions.question: What is tick rate in multiplayer games?
questions.answer: Frequency at which game server updates world state (e.g., 20Hz, 60Hz), affecting responsiveness and bandwidth.

questions.question: What is client-side prediction in multiplayer?
questions.answer: Client predicts movement locally before server confirms, masking lag (common in shooters). Server later reconciles corrections.

questions.question: What is interpolation vs extrapolation in networking?
questions.answer: Interpolation smooths between past states; extrapolation projects future states (less accurate). Both reduce visual jitter.

questions.question: What is a lag compensation?
questions.answer: Rewinding time on server to check hit registration at client's shot moment, reducing impact of latency (used in FPS games).

questions.question: What is the difference between authoritative server and peer-to-peer?
questions.answer: Authoritative server decides final state (anti-cheat). Peer-to-peer sends data between players (faster, cheat-prone).

questions.question: What is a hitbox?
questions.answer: Invisible collider (often simplified shape) attached to character for damage detection, larger or smaller than visual mesh.

questions.question: What is a hurtbox?
questions.answer: Region on character that receives damage when hitbox overlaps it (separate from collision avoidance).

questions.question: What is a weapon sway?
questions.answer: Camera/weapon motion responding to player movement, adding realism in FPS games.

questions.question: What is a first-person controller vs third-person controller?
questions.answer: First-person: camera at eye level, arms/weapons visible. Third-person: camera behind character, requiring character animations visible.

questions.question: What is root motion?
questions.answer: Character movement driven by animation (e.g., walking forward pushes position), not by script, for realistic foot sliding prevention.

questions.question: What is inverse kinematics (IK)?
questions.answer: Computing joint rotations to achieve end-effector position (e.g., feet on uneven ground, hands grabbing object).

questions.question: What is a blend tree in animation?
questions.answer: Blends multiple animations based on parameters (speed, direction) for smooth transitions between walk, run, idle.

questions.question: What is a retargeting in animation?
questions.answer: Transferring animation from one character skeleton to another of different proportions using mapping.

questions.question: What is a cutscene (cinematic) in games?
questions.answer: Non-interactive sequence advancing story, either real-time (in-engine) or pre-rendered video.

questions.question: What is a quick time event (QTE)?
questions.answer: Gameplay requiring pressing button shown on screen within time limit during cutscene, creating interactivity.

questions.question: What is a branching narrative?
questions.answer: Story with player choices affecting dialogue, character fate, or ending, tracked via flags or variables.

questions.question: What is a dialogue tree?
questions.answer: UI structure showing conversation options with NPCs, branches based on prior choices or player stats.

questions.question: What is an inventory system?
questions.answer: Data structure (array/list/dictionary) holding player items, often with UI grid, stacking, equipping, and weight limits.

questions.question: What is a skill tree (talent tree)?
questions.answer: Progression system where players spend points unlocking abilities/perks, often in hierarchical branches.

questions.question: What is a crafting system?
questions.answer: Combining resource items to create new items (e.g., wood + stone = axe) using recipes.

questions.question: What is a save system (serialization) in games?
questions.answer: Persisting game state (player position, inventory, quest flags) to disk using JSON, binary, or SQLite.

questions.question: What is a checkpoints vs autosave?
questions.answer: Checkpoints fixed positions (respawn points). Autosave saves entire state periodically or at specific events.

questions.question: What is a difficulty curve?
questions.answer: Gradual increase of challenge as player progresses (tutorial → easier → harder), balancing frustration vs boredom.

questions.question: What is a dynamic difficulty adjustment (DDA)?
questions.answer: System automatically tuning difficulty based on player performance (e.g., more health packs if dying repeatedly).

questions.question: What is a procedural content generation (PCG)?
questions.answer: Algorithmic generation of levels, maps, items, or quests using noise (Perlin), L-systems, or wave function collapse.

questions.question: What is a seed in procedural generation?
questions.answer: Initial value ensuring deterministic random generation, allowing sharing same world (e.g., Minecraft seeds).

questions.question: What is a Octree vs Quadtree?
questions.answer: Quadtree subdivides 2D space; Octree subdivides 3D. Used for spatial partitioning, collision detection, and LOD.

questions.question: What is a spatial hash?
questions.answer: Grid-based data structure mapping objects to cells for fast neighbor queries, simpler than quadtree.

questions.question: What is a fixed-point arithmetic in games?
questions.answer: Using integers with implied decimal point (e.g., 1.23 = 123 << 2) for deterministic cross-platform physics without floating-point errors.

questions.question: What is a floating-point determinism issue?
questions.answer: Different CPU architectures or compiler optimizations causing small floating-point errors, leading to desync in multiplayer deterministic lockstep.

questions.question: What is a lockstep protocol in RTS games?
questions.answer: All clients process same inputs deterministically, only exchanging player commands, reducing bandwidth (used in Age of Empires).

questions.question: What is a shader warmup?
questions.answer: Pre-compiling shaders before gameplay to avoid hiccups during first use, common in console/PC games.

questions.question: What is a draw call?
questions.answer: CPU command telling GPU to render mesh with material/texture. High draw calls (1000s) reduce performance; batching reduces draws.

questions.question: What is batch rendering (static/dynamic)?
questions.answer: Combining multiple meshes into one draw call: static batching (unchanged objects), dynamic batching (moving, limited vertex count).

questions.question: What is a LOD group (Unity) / LOD Actor (Unreal)?
questions.answer: Manages multiple LOD levels for group of objects, swapping them based on distance for performance.

questions.question: What is mipmapping?
questions.answer: Pre-generating texture downsamples (mip levels) reducing aliasing and improving GPU cache when texture is far.

questions.question: What is an anisotropy filtering?
questions.answer: Improves texture sharpness when viewed at oblique angles (e.g., ground textures), reducing blur.

questions.question: What is anti-aliasing (AA)?
questions.answer: Techniques reducing jagged edges (aliasing): FXAA (post-process fast but blurry), MSAA (multisampling quality), TAA (temporal smooth).

questions.question: What is motion blur?
questions.answer: Post-processing effect simulating camera blur during fast movement, enhancing realism but can cause motion sickness.

questions.question: What is bloom effect?
questions.answer: Light bleeding from bright areas creating glow, simulating camera overexposure.

questions.question: What is HDR rendering (High Dynamic Range)?
questions.answer: Using higher color precision (floating point) before tone mapping to LDR, preserving bright highlights and dark details.

questions.question: What is tone mapping?
questions.answer: Converting HDR color values to LDR display range, applying curves (ACES, Reinhard) for cinematic look.

questions.question: What is a skybox?
questions.answer: A cube with textured inner faces rendering distant environment (sky, clouds, stars), always centered on camera.

questions.question: What is a reflection probe (cubemap)?
questions.answer: Captures environment reflections for shiny materials, updates dynamically or baked offline.

questions.question: What is a lightmap?
questions.answer: Pre-baked static lighting texture storing diffuse and shadow data, saving real-time lighting performance.

questions.question: What is a real-time global illumination (GI) like Enlighten / Lumen?
questions.answer: Dynamic bounce lighting updating in real-time (Lumen in UE5) for moving lights and objects, contrasted with baked GI.

questions.question: What is a physics material?
questions.answer: Defines surface properties like friction (slip resistance) and bounciness (restitution) for collisions.

questions.question: What is a ragdoll effect?
questions.answer: Replacing animated character with physics-driven rigidbodies on death for realistic limp falling.

questions.question: What is spring arm / camera lag?
questions.answer: Smoothing camera movement following character with delay (lag) and collision avoidance, preventing clipping.

questions.question: What is a post-processing stack?
questions.answer: Collection of screen-space effects applied after rendering: bloom, depth of field, color grading, vignette.

questions.question: What is depth of field (DoF) in games?
questions.answer: Blurring background/foreground simulating camera focus, emphasizing subject.

questions.question: What is a skeletal animation?
questions.answer: Skinning mesh vertices to bone hierarchy; transforming bones to animate characters (smooth deformations).

questions.question: What is a skeletal mesh vs static mesh?
questions.answer: Skeletal mesh has bones and skinning for animation; static mesh for non-moving objects (walls, crates).

questions.question: What is an animation blend space?
questions.answer: 2D parameter space mixing multiple animations (e.g., forward speed vs direction strafe).

questions.question: What is additive animation?
questions.answer: Layering animations on base pose (e.g., aim offset additive to walk cycle) for modular animation.

questions.question: What is an animation mask?
questions.answer: Selecting which bones are affected by animation (e.g., upper body shooting while lower body running).

questions.question: What is a navmesh obstacle?
questions.answer: Dynamic obstacle blocking AI pathfinding, updating navmesh at runtime (e.g., doors, moving crates).

questions.question: What is an occlusion area (precomputed visibility) vs dynamic occlusion culling?
questions.answer: Precomputed area marks which cells see each other. Dynamic occlusion culling (GPU-based) computes real-time visibility (e.g., Unity Umbra).
